ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for economic beehoon with otah, chicken wing and egg. Mapped to rice vermicelli dish as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (rice noodles, cooked) used as the closest defensible anchor for this composite Singaporean hawker dish. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for economic beehoon with otah, chicken wing and egg.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT code identified for this composite hawker dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker plate of economic beehoon with otah, chicken wing and egg weighs approximately 300 g, comprising a base of stir-fried rice vermicelli (~150 g), one piece of grilled otah (~40 g), one deep-fried chicken wing (~80 g), and one egg (~30 g).

nutrient_source.title: Economic Beehoon with Otah, Chicken Wing and Egg — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Economic Beehoon with Otah, Chicken Wing and Egg (cr_id F05060054). All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from component analysis using USDA FoodData Central data for rice vermicelli, otah, fried chicken wing, and egg.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles, cooked — Glycemic Index Database; analogous estimate applied to economic beehoon composite dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for economic beehoon with otah, chicken wing and egg as a composite dish. GI of 61 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked rice noodles/rice vermicelli (GI ~61, medium category), which forms the dominant carbohydrate source in this dish. The protein and fat from otah, chicken wing, and egg will moderately attenuate postprandial glucose response. GL per serving calculated as: GI (61) × carbohydrate per serving (65.0 g from 300 g portion) / 100 = 39.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 481 mg/100g (1443 mg per 300 g serving) substantially exceeds the 400 mg/100g threshold, driven by otah fish paste, fried chicken wing marinade, and soy sauce seasoning.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit this dish to small portions or avoid it; request reduced seasoning and avoid adding soy sauce or chilli sauce at the table, as a single serving provides approximately 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2400 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice vermicelli base provides 21.68 g carbohydrate/100g with only 0.79 g dietary fibre/100g, yielding a medium-to-high glycaemic load of ~39.7 per serving; deep-fried chicken wing adds saturated fat that worsens insulin resistance.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should reduce portion size to no more than 150 g, choose a smaller chicken wing or substitute with steamed protein, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to lower the overall glycaemic load and attenuate postprandial glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (481 mg/100g), saturated fat from deep-fried chicken wing and coconut milk in otah, and the absence of protective dietary fibre or potassium create a combined cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to its high sodium and saturated fat content; if consumed, request grilled chicken instead of deep-fried, limit otah to one piece, and avoid additional condiments to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried chicken wing generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; sodium at 481 mg/100g exceeds the RA threshold.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid the deep-fried chicken wing component and request grilled or steamed chicken instead; otah's turmeric and lemongrass provide minor anti-inflammatory benefit but do not offset the AGE and sodium burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Economic Beehoon with Otah, Chicken Wing and Egg is a popular Singaporean hawker dish served at coffee shops, school canteens, and food courts across the island. The dish consists of stir-fried rice vermicelli (beehoon) as the carbohydrate base, accompanied by a piece of grilled otah (a spiced fish paste cake wrapped in banana leaf and grilled over charcoal or a gas flame), one deep-fried chicken wing, and a cooked egg (typically fried or hard-boiled). The beehoon is stir-fried with light soy sauce, oil, and sometimes bean sprouts or cabbage, giving it a savoury, slightly smoky flavour. Otah contributes a fragrant, spicy, and slightly sweet taste from its blend of fish paste, coconut milk, lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, and chilli. The deep-fried chicken wing adds a crispy, rich, and savoury element. Per 100 g, the dish provides 178 kcal, 6.53 g protein, 7.13 g fat, 21.68 g carbohydrate, 1.43 g sugar, 0.79 g dietary fibre, and 481 mg sodium. The high sodium content (481 mg/100g) makes this dish a concern for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. The refined carbohydrate base with low fibre and a medium GI of approximately 61 poses a moderate-to-high glycaemic risk for individuals with type 2 diabetes. The dish is commonly considered halal and is a staple of the Singaporean economic rice/noodle tradition, where diners select affordable side dishes to accompany a noodle or rice base.
common_user_queries: How many calories in economic beehoon with otah and chicken wing?
Is beehoon with otah suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat beehoon with chicken wing if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05060054) with a reported confidence of 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and documented accordingly.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.1 g/100g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central component data for rice vermicelli, grilled otah, deep-fried chicken wing, and egg, as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. This value should be replaced with a direct HPB measurement when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite hawker dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on cooked rice noodles/rice vermicelli from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. 4. The input record incorrectly listed primary_cuisine as 'western' and ethnic_tags as ['western']; corrected to 'Singaporean' based on the dish's cultural origin and HPB classification. 5. Serving size of 300 g is an estimate for a standard hawker plate; actual portion weights vary by stall.
